content.text: Soniya was a little girl with an unshakable love for fantasy stories. She spent her days buried in books, dreaming of magical worlds far beyond her own. One quiet afternoon, as she lay thinking about the stories she adored, she heard a strange sound. Startled, she looked around and was astonished to find a wall speaking to her.

The wall called her name softly, inviting her closer with a playful smile. Curious and slightly nervous, Soniya approached. The wall asked her to place her hand upon it. The moment she did, a small opening appeared, pulling her gently into another world—a world hidden behind walls.

Inside, everything was new and fascinating. Soon, a small wall-shaped creature approached her and spoke:
"Do you remember me? I’ve been waiting for you to talk to me all these days. Now you are my friend."

Though frightened at first, Soniya grew fond of this little wall. They became close friends, and one day, the little wall revealed why she had been brought there. “Our King asked me to bring you. He needs your help.”

Together they went to the King, who was as tall as a giant. He explained that the Queen had mysteriously disappeared, and according to their ancient rule, only children around the age of ten could enter Wall World. Soniya had been chosen.

Still unsure how she could help, Soniya began exploring with her wall-friend. While wandering, they noticed a strange creature, something that moved like a cat but had human-like hands and feet. They followed it for days, each time catching only a glimpse before it vanished. Finally, with a clever plan, the wall-friend managed to stop the creature. To their surprise, it was not a monster at all, but a two-year-old baby.

Soniya soon realized it was none other than her own little brother! Hidden behind this mystery was the Queen, who adored children but had been secretly keeping the baby with her. In Wall World, children were not usually allowed, yet the Queen cherished playing with them and had brought Soniya’s brother into her secret world.
The truth revealed, the King thanked Soniya. “You are always welcome here. This is your world too.”

Just as happiness filled her heart, the ground shook violently. Soniya suddenly woke to find her mother gently shaking her. “Wake up, dear, it’s time for school.”

Her brother was playing nearby, safe and sound. It had all been a dream. Yet, the joy of that dream lingered in her heart as she walked to school with a smile.
Moral of the Story

Happiness does not always come from reality or possessions—it can bloom even in a dream, in a thought, or in a moment of imagination. What matters is how we embrace it. If a little dream can bring so much joy, why spend life in sadness? Choose to be happy, always.

The life of Asha Kumari had always carried with it a smell of Dettol and boiled rice. Ask her and she would have told you that she had spent her childhood within the high crumbling sections of Shanti Ashram - a quasi home as it liked to think that it was but felt more like a waiting room of unwanted children.

Discipline was actually the thing next to lentils at Shanti Ashram. On the occasion of someone letting a blanket spread, Sister Pramila could take off her slippers and run at a speed that made even a cricket ball lag behind. Asha learned how to fold a blanket so that it could cut a finger. She got a job at Call Centre Services, a glass box of a call centre, where no one could escape the smell of cheap deodorant, stale coffee and fake enthusiasm. The same navy blue salwar, hair cut in a tight pony to cause her a headache, and the id card neatly clipped in a straight manner were also evident.

Initially she was an ideal employee. Hello ma’am, what can I assist you with today? I see your concern sir, hold on the line. She ticked all her boxes, made out all her reports, wished everyone. Good Evening right out of the book. Still nobody had told Asha that beyond the walls of stone that hemmed in the Ashram, freedom was a slippery thing.

It began by a cold. Thursday morning Asha woke up with a runny nose and said to herself: I might go in--or I might not go in. And she drew the blanket over her head and went to sleep until noon. When she eventually slunk into the run-down kitchen of her PG, she got an eyebrow raise by her flat mate Pinky, who was selling fake handbags online.
“You bunked?” Pinky asked, half admiring, half scandalized.
Asha simply shrugged. A packet of Maggi into the kettle and she grinned at her very own self.

The following day, she had returned to her cubicle, headset, false smile and copy paste politeness. Yet an itch was set on foot. The following week she sent this to her manager: Cannot make it, ma’am family problem. No family (Existed). She was in a park the whole day, lying on the grass, eating street samosas and laughing at passing dogs. This time she was not concerned about the folding of her clothes, ironing her dupatta, washing her plate. She was grungy and she felt vibrant.
A missed day led to two and then three. Mr. Mehta (the man who wore the same red Queenie tie and believed that he was the boss of the universe) called her in.
Okay Asha, this is not school. You cannot bunk.”
I do know Sir.
It is unacceptable. Are you interested in this job?”
She failed to do so.

They dismissed her two days afterwards. She stuffed her broken water bottle, clear plastic lunchbox and got out of call Centre, never to come back. No job. No plan. No Ashram wall to keep her straight.

She walked until her shoes hurt, and she would sit on a bench close to a dusty construction site. That is when she noticed him, a gentleman in fresh linen with the phone against his shoulder and his hands gesturing Format to unseen blueprints. He appeared to own clouds.

Yes, the hill plot, said he; I want the house finished before the rains. Till then I shall visit you once a month and that is all. Asha stood and watched him go in his bright car leaving behind half a dreamed life made of steel rods and fresh cement.
That evening she climbed the hill and forced open the wooden gate. No watchman. No locks. Only a house partly built and broadly opens to the stars.
So she moved in.

She was sweeping the floors with a busted broom. Put a cardboard and old curtain into a bed. Tea that has been boiled over a little gas range which she borrowed of Pinky who said she was crazy but never mind.

In vacant rooms, Asha danced as well as drew lyrics on the naked concrete walls. She observed the rise of sun through gaps that would be holding windows one day. She would hear the crunch of wheels of cars once a month and she would hide behind stacks of bricks or would sneak out and buy groceries.
Then one evening she stumbled over a paint bucket, literally, and had to look up at the unbelieving face of the owner.
You-- What the- Who are you?" he yelled.
Asha sat up, hair sticking out like a mad scientist’s. “I’m Asha. Do you want tea? It’s slightly on burnt.”
“Tea? In my house?”
“Technically it’s only half your house. The other half is still a beautiful ruin- I’m helping”
“Helping?! You are trespassing! This is my property!”
“Well, private property should really have a lock, or at least a door.”
He gazed at her. “You broke in?”
She looked offended. “I entered. Gently. Like fresh air.”
He stomped further in, waving at the mess- clothesline tied between two pillars, an old radio balanced on bricks.
“You have laundry here?! Is that my bed sheet?”
He rubbed his forehead. “How long have you been living here?”
Asha counted on her fingers. “One month, two weeks, plus four days. And a half.”
“A half?”
“Well yesterday I want down to the market so technically-”

He was called Raghav Singh. He had a plan, a blueprint, a spreadsheet of everything, an idea which lays down every brick of his perfect concrete, until this girl that he found sat in his perfectly half-started living room and turned his perfect concrete into a circus tent.
He threw his hands up. “Get out. Pack your, your circus and leave now.”
Asha hugged her knees, putting on her best tragic face. “My distant cousin promised I could stay with her, but she ran away with a yoga teacher. So technically you’re all I have left, Raghav ji.”
Raghav threw up his hands again. “What kind of cousin runs away with a yoga teacher?!”
She sighed dramatically. “Flexible people are very unreliable sir.”
Raghav just blinked.
Asha shuffled closer. “Just one month. I’ll find my cousin- or another cousin- or someone’s cousin. I promise I’ll arrange a real place. Please.”
Raghav sighed, and muttered, “God help me.” He looked at her, defeated. “One month, if you’re still here after that, I’m calling the police.”
Asha’s grin spread so wide it nearly fell off her place. “Deal!”
She made him burnt parathas and narrated to him about Shanti Ashram, about the flying slipper of Sister Pramila and about her call centre where she never uttered a natural voice.

He was extending his duration of stay again every month. She would be using kitchen spoons to paint a door sometimes since she had no brushes. She would sometimes fall asleep on the floor at the centre of a circle of half-started poems.
One day he said to her, looking at her half-painted door, half-broken gas stove, half-scribbled walls, etc., etc. “So you never finish anything?”
Asha simply smiled with her hair messily put in a bun, and a smudge of paint on her nose. “What’s good in finishing?”
He attempted to get angry. He did. But then as he saw her dance around singing out of tune in an empty kitchen, Raghav knew he was not in too much of a hurry to finish anything either.

Another evening they were sitting on the floor and eating half-cooked maggi biscuits and watching fireflies go through the broken window frame into the room. She rested her head on his shoulder comforted and warm.
“Do you miss your old life?” he asked softly.
Asha shook her head. “No walls to fold blankets behind. No slippers flying at my head. No fake Good Evenings. Just this- half a house, half a plan, and a full heart.”
Raghav stared at her and the cracks of his own precise world got illuminated.
As Raghav took a few steps nearer the half-built walls appeared to lean towards him. Asha glanced at him green-eyed, with that inexpressibly lazy grin, hurly-burly hair and all.
“Someday, this house will be done, you know,” he teased, brushing a paint flake from her cheek.
She only laughed, putting her head against his shoulder. “Maybe. But then what is there in the doing, Raghav? Things that are done are dead of mischief.”
He looked around at the broken windows, the scattered bricks, the dreams that still smelled of wet cement. And somehow, it all felt alive — exactly like her.
There they stood in that half-built house on a name-less hill -- two half-finished stories, very well satisfied never to be finished.

Much later, when the roof was half done and the walls still argued with the wind, Raghav would sometimes catch her dancing barefoot on the cement floor, singing off-key to a radio she’d fixed with tape. He’d stand at the doorway, arms folded, pretending to scold. “One day you’ll have to finish something, you know.”
And Asha would spin, laughing, sweeping along, the hair, the eyes shining. “Why? It is half-done and that is just right. Like me. As we are.”

And before he would rise up a protest, she took him in her dance -- the girl who could fold her bed sheet like a knife was laughing barefoot in a house of half-made walls and was teaching him that unfinished is sometimes the best place to be.

And the wind would whistle through the open walls, and their laughter out to the hills, and no locks, no walls tight, no ends too tidy. And Asha would whisper in his ear, “What’s so good in finishing, anyway?”
"November is the hush before winter's song, where every leaf whispers its last secret to the earth."

It all happened in the month of November. Wernix was a boy who loved adventures and lived near a beautiful forest. Every night, he kept dreaming about a soft voice calling his name, "Find me... Help me..." That voice sounded like it came from the forest. Every morning, he woke up with the same feeling: he had to go there and see for himself.

One fine evening, when the sky was orange and the air was cool, he finally decided to find out what was inside the forest and why he kept dreaming about it. He walked through the trees, listening carefully. The forest was all quiet, with only the sound of leaves moving in the wind. Then he saw a small rabbit. Wernix started to chase it playfully but the rabbit suddenly jumped into a bush. When Wernix followed, he found something he never expected.

Behind the bush was a secret place. The trees were taller, the air was pure and a silver waterfall shone in the distance. It was like he had stepped into another world. At the waterfall, he saw a strange creature--her eyes were bright like the moon, her hair flowed like the river, and a crown of flowers rested on her head. "At last, you came" she said softly. "I'm Lispa, Queen of Goblins and also the protector of this beautiful forest," she introduced herself. Wernix felt like she knew why he had a dream about the forest and why he came to the forest, so without saying anything he carefully listened to her. She told Wernix that long ago, the amulet of the forest was stolen and without it the trees and rivers were losing life. But something worse was happening too--a dark shadow creature was also searching for the amulet. She also said that if it gets the amulet first, the forest would fall into darkness forever.

Lispa looked at him with hope. "Only someone who is chosen can help me, and you are the chosen" she said. Wernix, his heart brimming with a sense of adventure, said without hesitation, “I will help you”. He promised to help her. But to help her, he had to change his appearance like her. So Lispa touched his shoulder, and in an instant, Wernix was no longer human. He had become a goblin, small, quick and he was able to feel that the forest needed him.

They started their journey to find the amulet. They travelled through deep caves, crossed rivers and climbed stone cliffs. A strange voice reached Wernix’s ears so clearly that he couldn’t ignore it; he listened carefully and followed it. But they didn't know that the shadow creature secretly followed them. When they reached near the end of the forest, it was filled with darkness but the shadow creature was always close behind them. The creature between the trees, eyes glowing red, waited for the right moment to steal the amulet. They found an old tree, "It's the heart of the forest", said Lispa. Now, Wernix strongly heard the voice saying "you found it, come closer." As Wernix reached for it, the shadow creature appeared, roaring with anger.

Lispa struck the creature with all her strength, but it did nothing; the creature was too powerful, and her efforts only drained her. Only the Chosen had the power to defeat it. While Lispa was distracting the creature, Wernix rushed and grabbed the amulet. He felt its warmth and the forest itself seemed to fight back. The trees moved, the ground shook, and the super magical power came out from the amulet. Wernix lifted the amulet towards the creature but it moved and hurt Lispa who was behind the creature. With the power of the amulet, Wernix flew high in the sky and powerfully came down, attacking the creature on his head. He could sense, through the amulet, the source of the creature’s power—a glowing red stone hidden inside its skull. The amulet guided him, showing him exactly how to use its magical energy. Summoning all his strength, Wernix shattered the red stone, the shadow creature screamed and vanished.

He quickly rushed to Lispa’s side, seeing her hurt and bleeding, his heart pounding with worry. He gave the amulet to her. It healed her with a bright light coming out of it. Finally, peace returned. She smiled at him, her eyes full of trust. "You have done what no human has ever done", she said. With a gentle touch, she changed him back into a human. But this time, she placed the amulet in his hand.

"It belongs to me", she said, "but the forest chooses you so you must have it, as long as it's with you, our forest will always be peaceful and safe", she said. Wernix held it closely, knowing his life would never be the same. The forest slowly faded behind him, but Lispa's voice stayed in his heart. And every November, he returned, not just as an adventurer but as the only human the Goblin Queen trusted and also hoping to see her again.

Rex was a man who knew the world only by giving. Every morning, he wore a smile as if it were armor, hiding the storms that raged within. He helped everyone neighbors, friends, strangers alike—lifting spirits, sharing laughs, and offering gentle words. Yet, no one ever stopped to ask how he was, or if the man behind the laughter even knew what joy felt like.

Every night, when the world went to sleep, Rex sat by his window, staring at the cold, silent streets. He let the tears come then quiet, endless rivers tracing the lines of a face that no one truly saw. The moonlight fell softly on his hands, trembling as they covered a heart that had never known the warmth of love. In the morning, he would laugh again, as if the tears had been mere shadows of a forgotten dream.

He remembered the days when he had hoped for friendship, for a love that would stay, for someone to see the man beyond the laughter. But those dreams had quietly slipped away, leaving him with only echoes of his own voice, speaking into emptiness. And so, he learned to hide, to pretend, to paint happiness on a canvas of sorrow.

One day, a child asked him, “Why do you always smile, sir?” Rex knelt down, looking into the curious eyes, and whispered, “Sometimes, my dear, a smile is all we have to protect the world from the pain we carry inside.” The child nodded, unaware that the words were not a lesson but a confession.

Rex walked home alone that evening, the streets silent, the wind carrying whispers of a world that had forgotten him. He wept quietly, not from despair alone, but from the beauty he had given to others that had never returned to him. And in that silence, he understood something profound: the world could never see the depths of a heart that only existed to give, unless it learned to look beyond the surface.
And so Rex lived, a lonely man in a crowded world, teaching the unknowing the value of love and kindness, even when no one returned it.
Moral & Quote:
“The deepest pain often hides behind the brightest smile, but kindness given from a lonely heart is never wasted—it plants seeds of love in ways we may never see.”
